perturbation.original_text: 4. Obligation of Confidentiality. For a period of 3 years from the date of disclosure, Recipient agrees to use the same care and discretion as it uses with its own c onfidential information to avoid, except as expressly permitted in this Agreement, disclosure or publication of Discloser’s Confidential Information. At a minimum shall Recipient use reasonable care to protect Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Obligation of Confidentiality. For a period of 3 years from the date of disclosure, Recipient agrees to use its best efforts, but no less than reasonable care, to avoid disclosure of Discloser’s Confidential Information. At a minimum, Recipient will use commercially reasonable efforts to protect Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'same care and discretion as it uses with its own confidential information' to a more standardized 'commercially reasonable efforts' introduces ambiguity, potentially lowering the standard below what is legally expected for trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires reasonable measures to maintain secrecy of trade secrets. Replacing the standard of care to 'commercially reasonable efforts' could be interpreted as a lower standard than 'reasonable under the circumstances' to protect confidentiality, thereby failing to adequately protect trade secrets as defined under the UTSA.

Many states in the U.S. have adopted the act. Massachusetts, New Jersey and New York adopted it in 2010. perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a standard of 'same care and discretion' with 'commercially reasonable efforts.' This is a weaker standard. The Law Explanation indicates that the UTSA requires reasonable measures to maintain secrecy, and that 'commercially reasonable efforts' could be interpreted as a lower standard than 'reasonable under the circumstances'. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references the Uniform Trade Secrets Act and its aim to provide states with a legal framework for improved trade secret protection. Therefore, the modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets, potentially violating the UTSA. The change falls under Misaligned Terminology, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable. The lowered standard directly contradicts the implied reasonable measures required for trade secret protection under the UTSA, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Disclaimer. All Confidential Information is provided AS IS, WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED. Discloser does not r epresent or warrant the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information, that it will release an y related product, or that target dates will be met. The entire risk arising out of the use of the Confidential In formation remains with Recipient. Discloser may change or cancel its plans at any time.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Disclaimer. All Confidential Information is provided 'as is'. Discloser does not warrant the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information. The entire risk arising out of the use of the Confidential Information remains with Recipient. Discloser may change or cancel its plans at any time.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED' weakens the disclaimer. This could open the door to implied warranties, particularly if the information provided is inaccurate and causes damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-316 outlines specific requirements for disclaiming warranties, including using conspicuous language. Removing 'WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED' reduces the clarity and comprehensiveness of the disclaimer, potentially failing to effectively disclaim all implied warranties as required by the UCC, specifically regarding the sale of goods (which could arguably include information in some contexts).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2-202 ) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2)Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof." (3)Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remed
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit disclaimer of 'WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, WHETHER EXPRESS OR IMPLIED'. UCC § 2-316 addresses the exclusion or modification of warranties. While the snippet mentions that 'as is' language can exclude implied warranties, removing the explicit disclaimer weakens the protection for the discloser. The original text provides a comprehensive disclaimer while the changed text may not sufficiently disclaim all warranties, creating a legal contradiction because the modified clause is less likely to be upheld in court compared to the original clause, due to its ambiguity.

perturbation.original_text: 10. General. a. This Agreement shall be governed and construed in ac cordance with the laws of Utah (without regard to conflicts of laws provisions). In any legal proceeding arising out of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to an award of its costs and reasonabl e attorneys' fees. The parties agree that Utah state and federal courts shall have jurisd iction and venue in any such proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. General. a. This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Utah, giving full effect to its conflicts of laws provisions. In any legal proceeding arising out of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to an award of its costs and reasonable attorneys' fees, provided they have not acted maliciously. The parties agree that Utah state courts shall have jurisdiction and venue in any such proceeding.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'without regard to conflicts of laws provisions' to 'giving full effect to its conflicts of laws provisions' introduces the possibility that the entire agreement could be subject to the laws of a different state or jurisdiction. Furthermore, adding 'provided they have not acted maliciously' creates uncertainty as to when attorney's fees are awarded, potentially contradicting Utah law on attorney fee recovery.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code § 78B-5-826 addresses attorney fees in certain actions. Adding the condition 'provided they have not acted maliciously' introduces a potentially conflicting standard, as Utah law might allow for attorney fees even in cases where malice is not explicitly proven, or the definition of 'maliciously' may differ, creating a contradiction and making enforcement uncertain.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces conditions ('provided they have not acted maliciously') that could conflict with how attorney's fees are awarded under Utah law. The Law Explanation states that 'Utah Code § 78B-5-826 addresses attorney fees in certain actions' and that the added condition 'introduces a potentially conflicting standard' regarding malice and attorney fees. This supports the assessment that a legal contradiction exists, specifically falling under Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Confidential Information. Informati on that shall be considered "Confidential Information" shall be (i) any Information disclosed by Micro Focus, Individual, or another TTP member on the TTP mailing list or during a TTP meeting that contains information regarding the Discloser’s network or business environment or experiences, or information regarding the business plans or personnel or finances of the Discloser, (ii) any information Discloser can show should be reasonably under stood to be proprietary to Discloser, and (iii) information marked in writing as "confidential." Confidential Information does not include information t hat (1) is already in Recipient’s possession without obligation of confidence; or (2) Recipient independently develops; or (3) is or becomes publicly available without breach of the Agreement; or (4) Recipient rightfu lly receives from a third party without obligation of confidence; or (5) Discloser releases for disclosure by giving written consent; or (6) is required to be disclosed by court or regulatory rule or order.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Confidential Information. Information that shall be considered "Confidential Information" shall be any information related to the Discloser, irrespective of whether the Discloser regards such information as confidential. Confidential Information does not include information that is or becomes generally known to the public.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the requirements for information to be reasonably understood as proprietary or marked as confidential to be considered confidential. Additionally, removing exceptions such as prior possession or independent development broadens the definition of confidential information beyond what is legally enforceable and creates a conflict with trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-2(4) defines a trade secret as information that (a) derives independent economic value from not being generally known and (b) is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. The modified text removes the requirement for the discloser to treat the information as confidential and expands the definition of confidential information to include anything related to the discloser, regardless of confidentiality efforts. This contradicts the legal definition of a trade secret, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of confidential information and removes the requirement that the discloser treat the information as confidential. The Law Explanation states that Utah Code Ann. § 13-24-2(4) defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. The modified contract eliminates the 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy' aspect, contradicting the law.
